title: Taming quantum interference: a route to high electrical conductance in carbon nanotube assemblies
authors: Teresa Kulka, Agnieszka E. Lekawa-Raus, John S. Bulmer, Krzysztof Koziol, Fedor F. Balakirev, Irina V. Lebedeva, Jacek A. Majewski, Magdalena Marganska, Karolina Z. Milowska
abstract: Miniaturized electronics require lightweight conductors that maintain high conductance under demanding conditions. CNT networks are promising candidates, but their transport is governed by inter-nanotube junctions where electron waves interfere. Controlling this interference requires understanding how junction architecture shapes transmission. We explore coherent transport through experimentally relevant junctions, from single and multiple single-walled CNT (SWCNT) contacts to double-walled CNT (DWCNT) and triple-walled CNT (TWCNT) junctions, with atomistic tight-binding non-equilibrium Green's-function calculations, also under a perpendicular magnetic field. We use analytically solvable minimal models to identify transport regimes expected for quasi-1D nanoscale junctions, and an electron-waveguide picture to interpret their CNT-specific manifestations. For single SWCNT--SWCNT junctions, high-transmission windows are set mainly by overlap length, doping and magnetic field. Gateway states can enhance conductance when some CNT subbands are gapped, and in some cases a magnetic field can restore transmission by lifting an interference blockade. In more complex architectures, added paths become selective: multi-junctions generate resonant filtering, while additional walls redistribute transmission instead of acting as independent channels. DWCNT junctions remain outer-wall dominated and SWCNT-like, whereas TWCNT junctions become genuinely multi-channel and more field-sensitive. This explains the lower, more field-sensitive conductance of multi-walled CNT (MWCNT) fibres, in accord with our ultrahigh-field measurements on SWCNT and MWCNT fibres. Ultimately, this work turns microscopic interference mechanisms into design principles for high-conductance, field-stable CNT conductors.
summary: This paper investigates how quantum interference at the junctions between carbon nanotubes affects the overall electrical conductivity of nanotube networks. By combining atomistic simulations with high-field experiments, the authors show that complex multi-walled structures can suffer from interference-induced conductance suppression, providing a blueprint for designing more stable and conductive CNT-based electronics.
main_problem: Understanding and controlling quantum interference at inter-nanotube junctions to achieve high electrical conductance and field stability in carbon nanotube (CNT) assemblies.
main_result: The study identifies that multi-walled CNT architectures introduce complex, field-sensitive interference that reduces conductance, whereas SWCNT junctions can be tuned via overlap length and magnetic fields to achieve high-transmission 'gateway' states.
method: The work combines atomistic tight-binding non-equilibrium Green's-function (NEGF) simulations and analytical waveguide models with experimental magnetotransport measurements on CNT fibers using pulsed magnetic fields up to 60 T.
model_or_system: The physical systems include SWCNT, DWCNT, and TWCNT junctions and fibers. Theoretical models include an atomistic tight-binding Hamiltonian with Peierls substitution for magnetic fields and a minimal 1D atomic chain model.
key_observables: Electrical conductance (G), specific conductivity (normalized by linear density), magnetoresistance (MR), and transmission amplitude (T).
important_parameters: Nanotube diameter, chirality, overlap length, doping, perpendicular magnetic field strength, and inter-tube coupling.
main_assumptions: Assumes current injection is primarily through outer shells in MWCNTs; neglects inelastic processes like electron-phonon scattering; assumes magnetic fields are present only in the scattering region.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate junction architectures (SWCNT, DWCNT, TWCNT), transport regimes in atomic chains (standing waves, gateway states), and transmission maps/spectra as a function of energy and magnetic field.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of junction-governed transport, presents theoretical models (atomic chains and waveguide analogy), details the atomistic NEGF simulations, compares these to experimental magnetotransport data in CNT fibers, and concludes with design principles for high-conductance conductors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Models of CNTs and CNT junction architectures considered in this work, with the chiralities of the constituent CNTs listed below. Isolated CNT building blocks are shown for (a) SWCNT, (e) DWCNT and (g) TWCNT, with the corresponding junction architectures used in the calculations shown in (b–d), (f) and (h). The pink zig-zag lines schematically indicate possible electron tunnelling paths between neighbouring SWCNTs, or between adjacent walls in MWCNTs. i) Cross-sectional and (j) side views of the MWCNT junction with the radius r, intertube distance d, and overlap length L marked.

Figure 2: Morphology and magnetotransport of SWCNT and MWCNT fibres. (a-d) SEM images of CNT fibres directly spun from the FC-CVD reactor. (a) The SWCNT-based fibre and (b) a fibre made of a mixture of MWCNTs of different numbers of walls. Corresponding higher-magnification images highlight the alignment of individual (c) SWCNTs and (d) MWCNTs within the fibres. (e) Change in conductance of a 3 mm long MWCNT fibre sample against a magnetic field measured at 1.495K. Previously reported data [17] for SWCNT fibre (3 mm-long) measured at 1.3 K are included for comparison. The inset shows magnetoresistance MR [%] as a function of magnetic field for both samples.

Figure 3: Transmission and transport regimes in overlapping atomic chains. (a) Sketch of the system, with t the hopping integral along each chain and t′ the hopping between chains α and β. (b) The energy bands of the individual chains and the two hybridized bands E± in the central region. The circles mark the incoming (k0), reflected (−k0) and transmitted (k0 in R) momenta at a given energy, as well as the ±k± at this energy in the junction. The light violet stripes mark the range of the gateway states (see text for description of the transport regimes). (c) Numerically calculated T(E, L) – transmission through the junction, as a function of energy E and junction length L. The fine...

Figure 4: Transport across an atomic chain junction in perpendicular magnetic field. (a) Sketch of the system with the orientation of ⃗B and the vector potential ⃗A(y). Hopping integrals along x are modified by the Peierls phase, opposite on chains α and β. (b) The electronic bands are shifted by the Peierls phase, +φ on chain α and −φ on chain β. In the junction the shifted bands cross only at k = 0, π, and only in the neighborhood of these points the electronic states of the two chains can hybridize. These energy ranges are also those where transport is facilitated by gateway states, and with increasing ⃗B they shift towards E = 0. (c) Numerically calculated T(E, φ) for a junction with 49...

Figure 5: Electron transport through simple junctions of single-walled CNTs (type A systems) under an external perpendicular magnetic field. The junction schematics above the panels show some of the possible electron paths (red arrows). (a–d) Oscillations of the computed zero-bias transmission as a function of electron energy E for (9,9)+(9,9) SWCNT junctions with overlap lengths of (a) 49.19 nm (200 unit cells) and (b) 196.76 nm (800 unit cells), and for (12,6)+(12,6) SWCNT junctions with overlap lengths of (c) 45.08 nm (40 unit cells) and (d) 180.33 nm (160 unit cells), shown for magnetic fields B up to 200 T in the energy window [-0.6,0] eV.
